div.head: The Minister in China (MacMurray) to the Secretary of State
div.p:
20. Following from American company [consul general,] Shanghai:Clarence E. Gauss was in charge of consulate general at Shanghai Sept. 8, 1926, to June 7, 1927, in absence of Consul General Edwin S. Cunningham.
“January 8, 6 p.m. Following sent after consulting ArmstrongCommander Eldred B. Armstrong, U. S. N., commanding the U. S. S. Asheville of the Asiatic Fleet. who is repeating same message to commander in chief:Admiral Clarence S. Williams, U. S. N., commander in chief, Asiatic Fleet.
In view of recent unexpected turn of events up river which obviously exceeds situation contemplated on point 2,See telegram No. 627, Dec. 19, 1926, from the Minister in China, Foreign Relations, 1926, vol. i, p. 662. it is recommended that in order to give adequate protection to foreign life and property at Shanghai and to attempt to prevent seizure of Settlement and cannonading foreign-occupied area by mob violence as at Hankow and Kiushu, landing forces Shanghai should be increased to available maximum immediately upon Sun Ch’uan-fang’sMilitary overlord of Provinces of Kiangsu, Kiangsi, Chekiang, Fukien, and Anhwei, and concurrently director general of Shanghai and Woosung Port Administration, allied with Peking Government. initial defeat or indications of any local disorders. Situation Shanghai at present outwardly quiet but highly inflammable upon initial defeat of Sun by Southern forces as is probable.
Events in Hankow [and] Kiukiang, give adequate indication of intention of radical element of Nationalist[s] in my telegram of December 30, 3 p.m.Not found in Department files. The Legation appreciates that no distinction as to nationalities will be possible at Shanghai, all foreign lives and interests being equally in danger in the event of mob disturbances.”
